title: Behave
subtitle: The Biology of Humans at Our Best and Worst
author: Robert M. Sapolsky
themes: Human behavior can only be understood by integrating multiple levels of analysis — from neurochemistry to culture
The brain does not have a single 'center' for anything — behavior emerges from interacting circuits
Free will, as traditionally conceived, is difficult to reconcile with modern neuroscience
Context is everything — the same brain produces vastly different behavior in different environments
We are capable of both the best and the worst; understanding the biology of both is essential
The frontal cortex is the most human part of the brain — and the last to mature
excerpt: This book is about the biology of violence, aggression, and competition, and the biology of cooperation, empathy, and morality. It asks: why do we do the things we do? And it answers that question by examining behavior from multiple perspectives — what happened a second before, what happened minutes to days before, what happened during childhood, and what happened in the evolutionary history of our species. The answer is never simple, but it is always biological.
